mechanism_narrative: Real GDP (GDPC1) grows at a positive quarterly rate, indicating expanding aggregate demand. Persistent positive growth reinforces expectations of corporate earnings expansion, driving S&P 500 positive returns over the following quarter.
failure_condition: Rank IC of GDPC1 quarterly growth vs 60-day forward returns falls below 0.03 over any 3-year rolling window, or the mean forward return in quarters with positive GDP growth is not significantly above zero (t-test p>0.05).

mechanism_narrative: When the federal funds rate rises above a threshold of 3.5%, it signals that the Fed is actively tightening monetary policy. Higher discount rates compress equity valuations, leading to negative S&P 500 returns over the subsequent 20 trading days.
failure_condition: In periods when FEDFUNDS exceeds 3.5%, the average 20-day forward SPX return is not negative, or the proportion of positive returns exceeds 50% over any 2-year window.

mechanism_narrative: Rising consumer prices (CPIAUCSL) combined with rising federal funds rate (FEDFUNDS) indicates the Fed is raising rates to combat inflation. This dual pressure erodes real earnings and compresses valuations, producing negative S&P 500 returns over 20 trading days.
failure_condition: When both conditions hold, the 20-day forward SPX return is positive in more than 40% of instances, or the average return is not significantly negative (one-sided t-test p>0.10) over any 3-year period.

mechanism_narrative: During a late-cycle expansion, two consecutive monthly declines in industrial production (INDPRO) signal the onset of a recessionary contraction. Falling output reduces corporate profits and investor confidence, leading to negative S&P 500 returns over the next 60 trading days.
failure_condition: After INDPRO declines for two consecutive months, the 60-day forward SPX return is positive in more than 50% of cases, or the mean return is not significantly lower than in non-trigger periods (two-sample t-test p>0.05).

mechanism_narrative: Rising total nonfarm payroll employment (PAYEMS) signals a strengthening labor market and rising aggregate income, which supports consumer spending and corporate earnings. This always-on trend mechanism predicts positive S&P 500 returns over the following 20 trading days.
failure_condition: The rank correlation between month-over-month PAYEMS change and 20-day forward SPX return is less than 0.02 over any 3-year rolling window, or the average forward return following positive payroll changes is not significantly above zero.

mechanism_narrative: When month-over-month growth in real retail and food services sales (RSAFS) exceeds 0.5%, it signals a surge in consumer spending. Strong demand boosts corporate revenues and investor sentiment, producing positive S&P 500 returns over the next 5 trading days.
failure_condition: In months with RSAFS growth above 0.5%, the 5-day forward SPX return is negative in more than 40% of instances, or the average return is not significantly positive (one-sided t-test p>0.10) over any 2-year window.

mechanism_narrative: Simultaneous increases in housing starts (HOUST) and real GDP (GDPC1) indicate broad-based demand strength in both consumer durables (housing) and total output. This joint expansion reinforces corporate profitability and drives positive S&P 500 returns over the next 60 trading days.
candidate_trigger: HOUST month-over-month growth > 0 AND GDPC1 quarterly growth > 0
failure_condition: When both HOUST and GDPC1 are positive, the 60-day forward SPX return is negative in more than 40% of cases, or the mean return is not significantly above the unconditional mean (t-test p>0.10) over any 3-year period.

mechanism_narrative: In a low unemployment regime (UNRATE below 4.5%), a further monthly decline of more than 0.2 percentage points signals an overheating labor market. This triggers expectations of wage inflation and Fed tightening, leading to negative S&P 500 returns over the next 20 trading days.
candidate_trigger: UNRATE declines by more than 0.2pp in a single month
failure_condition: When UNRATE < 4.5% and a decline >0.2pp occurs, the 20-day forward SPX return is positive in more than 50% of instances, or the average return is not significantly negative (one-sided t-test p>0.10) over any 3-year window.
